subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 3! You have met decimals before, but in this unit you will take a large step forward and become truly fluent with them. You will focus on two powerful skills: multiplying and dividing decimals by powers of 10, and rounding numbers to a chosen level of precision. Along the way you will use these skills to estimate and check whether an answer is sensible. These are not dusty classroom exercises – they are the everyday tools of scientists, shopkeepers, engineers and anyone who works with measurements and money.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 7 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain what a power of 10 is, including negative powers such as 10⁻¹ = 0.1.
Multiply any decimal by 10, 100, 1000 and beyond by moving the decimal point to the right.
Divide any decimal by powers of 10 by moving the decimal point to the left.
Round decimals to a given number of decimal places.
Round numbers to a given number of significant figures.
Use rounding and powers of 10 to estimate the result of a calculation.
Judge whether an exact answer is reasonable by comparing it with your estimate.
Apply these skills to real problems in measurement, money and science.

concepts.title: Understanding Powers of 10
concepts.explanation: Everything in this unit rests on powers of 10, so let us start there. A power of 10 is any number you can write as 10 raised to some whole-number power. The positive powers grow quickly: Do you see the pattern? The exponent tells you exactly how many zeros follow the 1. This simple rule makes mental arithmetic with powers of 10 fast and dependable. Each power is ten times larger than the one before it, so the numbers climb the ladder of place value one rung at a time.
concepts.example: Negative powers of 10 are just as important, because they describe the decimal places. A negative exponent means ‘one over that power’:

concepts.title: Multiplying Decimals by Powers of 10
concepts.explanation: Here is the golden rule: multiplying by a power of 10 moves the decimal point to the right, and the number of places it moves equals the exponent. Multiplying makes a number bigger, so the digits shift into larger place values. 3.456 × 10 = 34.56 (point moves 1 place right)

concepts.title: Dividing Decimals by Powers of 10
concepts.explanation: Division is the mirror image of multiplication, so the rule flips: dividing by a power of 10 moves the decimal point to the left, again by as many places as the exponent. Dividing makes a number smaller, so the digits shift into smaller place values. 345.6 ÷ 10 = 34.56 (point moves 1 place left)

concepts.title: Rounding to Decimal Places
concepts.explanation: Rounding means replacing a number with a nearby, simpler number that has fewer decimal places. We round because real measurements have limits, and because a long string of decimals is often more precise than is useful. The skill is to choose a sensible level of precision and land on the closest number at that level. The standard rule is short and always the same. Look at the first digit you are cutting off – the one just to the right of the place you are keeping:
concepts.example: If that digit is 5 or more, round up (increase the last kept digit by 1).

concepts.title: Rounding to Significant Figures
concepts.explanation: Significant figures (s.f.) give a way to say how precise a number is, counting from its first meaningful digit rather than from the decimal point. This is the language scientists and engineers use, because it captures precision no matter how large or small the number is. First you must be able to spot which digits are significant. The rules are systematic:
concepts.example: All non-zero digits are significant. In 2453, all four digits count.

concepts.title: Estimation and Approximation
concepts.explanation: Estimation brings all these skills together. To estimate, you round the numbers in a calculation to simple values, work out the easy sum, and use the result to judge whether an exact answer is sensible. Estimation is the mathematician's early-warning system: it catches the big mistakes, like a misplaced decimal point, before they cause trouble. To estimate 47.8 × 23.2, round both numbers: 50 × 20 = 1000. The exact answer is 1109.36, so 1000 is a good guide. More importantly, the estimate would instantly expose a…
concepts.example: For adding and subtracting, round to a convenient place. To estimate 387 + 456 + 234, round to the nearest hundred: 400 + 500 + 200 = 1100. The true sum, 1077, is close. For division, round to numbers that divide…

methods.title: How to multiply or divide by a power of 10
steps: Read the power of 10 and count its zeros – that is how many places to move.
Decide the direction: multiply moves the point right, divide moves it left.
Slide the decimal point that many places, keeping the digits in order.
Add zeros as place holders if you run out of digits, and a leading zero if needed.

methods.title: How to round to decimal places
steps: Identify how many decimal places you must keep.
Look at the very next digit to the right.
If it is 5 or more, round the last kept digit up; otherwise leave it.
Handle any carry (cascade) and keep meaningful trailing zeros.

methods.title: How to round to significant figures
steps: Count significant figures from the first non-zero digit.
Keep the required number of them.
Look at the next digit and apply the 5-or-more rule.
Use place-holder zeros to keep the number the right size.

methods.title: How to estimate a calculation
steps: Round each number to a simple, convenient value.
Do the easy calculation in your head.
Compare the estimate with the exact answer to check it is the right size.

methods.title: Round to decimal places
steps: Solution: Keep the hundredths (9) and look at the next digit, the thousandths (3).
Since 3 < 5, round down – leave the 9 as it is.
Answer: 67.89.

methods.title: A rounding cascade
methods.example: Round 156.4999 to the nearest hundredth (2 d.p.).
steps: Solution: Keep the hundredths (9) and look at the thousandths (9).
Since 9 ≥ 5, round up.
Rounding 156.49 up by 0.01 gives 156.50 – the carry ripples from the hundredths into the tenths.
Keep the meaningful zero: 156.50.

workedExamples.title: Multiply by a power of 10
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 100 = 10², so move the decimal point 2 places right. 3.7 → 37. → 370. We had to add a zero because we ran out of digits. Answer: 370.

workedExamples.title: Multiply a small decimal
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 10,000 = 10⁴, so move the point 4 places right. 0.0089 → 0.089 → 0.89 → 8.9 → 89. The leading zeros disappear as the point sweeps past. Answer: 89.

workedExamples.title: Divide by a power of 10
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 1000 = 10³, so move the point 3 places left. 234.5 → 23.45 → 2.345 → 0.2345. We add a leading zero for clarity. Answer: 0.2345.

workedExamples.title: Find the missing exponent
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ask how many places the point must move to turn 2.8 into 28,000. 2.8 → 28 → 280 → 2800 → 28,000 is 4 places right, so n = 4. Check: 2.8 × 10⁴ = 2.8 × 10,000 = 28,000. ✓

workedExamples.title: Round to decimal places
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Keep the hundredths (9) and look at the next digit, the thousandths (3). Since 3 < 5, round down – leave the 9 as it is. Answer: 67.89.

workedExamples.title: A rounding cascade
workedExamples.prompt: Round 156.4999 to the nearest hundredth (2 d.p.).
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Keep the hundredths (9) and look at the thousandths (9). Since 9 ≥ 5, round up. Rounding 156.49 up by 0.01 gives 156.50 – the carry ripples from the hundredths into the tenths. Keep the meaningful zero: 156.50.

workedExamples.title: Round to significant figures
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count from the first non-zero digit: 4, 5, 6 are the three significant figures. The next digit is 7, so round up. Answer: 0.00457. The leading zeros are not significant; they only show the size.

workedExamples.title: Estimate to check a calculation
workedExamples.prompt: Estimate 6.8 × 9.2, then say whether an exact answer of 62.56 is reasonable.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Round: 6.8 ≈ 7 and 9.2 ≈ 9, so the estimate is 7 × 9 = 63. The exact answer 62.56 is very close to 63, so it is reasonable. ✓ If the answer had been 6.256 or 625.6, the estimate would have warned us of a decimal-point error.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 9
workedExamples.prompt: Round 67.8934 to: a) the nearest tenth b) the nearest hundredth c) the nearest whole number.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 10
workedExamples.prompt: Round 0.07865 to: a) 2 d.p. b) 3 d.p. c) 4 d.p.
workedExamples.solution: a) 0.08, b) 0.079, c) 0.0787 (the fifth decimal digit is 5, round up).

workedExamples.title: Guided example 11
workedExamples.prompt: Round to 3 significant figures: a) 0.004567 b) 0.09876 c) 2456.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 12
workedExamples.prompt: If 2.8 × 10ⁿ = 28,000, find n, and explain how you counted the places.

activities.title: The Decimal Point Slide
activities.materials: a strip of paper, a bold marker, and a small paper clip.
steps: Write a decimal number with its digits well spaced, for example 3 . 4 5 6, leaving room to slide the point.
Clip the paper clip onto the decimal point so it can slide left and right.
Call out ‘times 100!’ and slide the clip two places right; read the new number aloud.
Call out ‘divide by 1000!’ and slide it three places left, adding zeros where needed.
Take turns with a partner calling out operations and reading the results.

activities.title: Metric Conversion Market
activities.materials: a ruler or tape measure, and household objects to measure.
steps: Measure five objects in centimetres, writing each length as a decimal.
Convert each length to millimetres by multiplying by 10 (move the point one place right).
Convert each length to metres by dividing by 100 (move the point two places left).
Make a small table showing each object in mm, cm and m.
Check one row with a partner: do all three numbers describe the same length?

activities.title: Rounding Card Game
activities.materials: paper cards, each showing a decimal number such as 5.847, 9.362, 0.0567 or 156.499.
steps: Shuffle the cards and place them face down in a pile.
Turn over a card and roll a die: 1 means round to the nearest whole number, 2 means 1 d.p., 3 means 2 d.p. (re-roll 4, 5, 6).
Both players round the number; the first correct answer keeps the card.
Watch for cascade cards like 156.499 – they catch people out!
The player with the most cards at the end wins.

activities.title: The Estimation Challenge (Family Game)
activities.materials: a shopping receipt or a list of prices, and paper.
steps: Cover the total on the receipt.
Each player estimates the total by rounding every price to the nearest whole shilling and adding.
Reveal the real total. Whose estimate was closest?
Talk about which prices were hardest to round and why.
Play again with a longer list to see if your estimates get sharper.

activities.title: Powers of 10 Scavenger Hunt
activities.materials: curiosity, and any books, labels or packets around the house.
steps: Hunt for numbers that are very large or very small – on food packets, bottles, or in books.
For each one, write it and estimate the nearest power of 10 (is it about 10², 10³, 10⁻³?).
Order your finds from smallest to largest power of 10.
Share the most surprising number you found with your family.
You will start to notice that powers of 10 are hiding on almost every label and sign around you.

activities.title: Investigation – The Vanishing Zeros
steps: Start with 0.0456 and keep multiplying by 10, writing each result: 0.456, 4.56, 45.6, and so on.
Watch carefully: at what point do the leading zeros disappear?
Now start with 4560 and keep dividing by 10. Where does a leading zero appear?
Write a sentence describing exactly when zeros appear and vanish as the point moves.
Predict what happens to 7.0 when you divide by 10 four times, then test your prediction.

rules.title: Powers of 10 Rule
rules.text: The exponent counts the zeros: 10 to the 3 = 1000 (three zeros). A negative exponent counts decimal places: 10 to the minus 3 = 0.001. 10 to the n times 10 to the minus n = 1 (they are reciprocals).

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: Multiply / Divide by Powers of 10 Multiply: move the decimal point RIGHT (the number gets bigger). Divide: move the decimal point LEFT (the number gets smaller). Move as many places as the exponent; add zeros to fill any gaps.

rules.title: Rounding Rule
rules.text: Find the place you are keeping, then look at the next digit to its right. 5 or more: round up. Less than 5: round down. Watch for a cascade, and keep meaningful zeros like 4.50.

terms: 10 raised to a whole-number power, e.g. 10³ = 1000
The raised number showing the power
A digit position after the decimal point
A meaningful digit, counted from the first non-zero digit
Replacing a number with a nearby, simpler one
An approximate answer found by rounding first
A number that multiplies with another to give 1
commonMistakes: Count the zeros in the power of 10
Use only the next digit to the right

reasoningPrompts.prompt: If 2.8 × 10ⁿ = 28,000, find n, and explain how you counted the places.
Round 999.996 to: a) the nearest tenth b) the nearest whole number c) the nearest ten.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: a) 1000.0 (hundredths digit 6 rounds up and cascades), b) 1000, c) 1000.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Estimate each by rounding to whole numbers, then say what error a wrong answer of ten times too big would look like: a) 6.8 × 9.2 b) 47.6 ÷ 7.9.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: a) 7 × 9 = 63 (exact 62.56); a ten-times error would look like 6.256 or 625.6. b) 48 ÷ 8 = 6 (exact ≈ 6.03); a ten-times error would look like 0.6 or 60.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A number rounds to 3.60 when given to 2 d.p. Write down the smallest and largest values it could have been.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Smallest 3.595, largest 3.604999… (usually written as up to but not including 3.605).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why 5.23 × 1000 needs a zero added, but 5.234 × 1000 does not.

selfAssessment: I can explain what a power of 10 is, including negative powers such as 10⁻¹ = 0.1.
I can multiply any decimal by 10, 100, 1000 and beyond by moving the decimal point to the right.
I can divide any decimal by powers of 10 by moving the decimal point to the left.
I can round decimals to a given number of decimal places.
I can round numbers to a given number of significant figures.
I can use rounding and powers of 10 to estimate the result of a calculation.
I can judge whether an exact answer is reasonable by comparing it with your estimate.
I can apply these skills to real problems in measurement, money and science.
